event: Diagnosed to have tropical chronic pancreatitis in 2002
Maintained on oral pancreatic enzyme therapy for exocrine pancreatic insufficiency
Presented to a medical center in India with similar pain and fever
CT scan of the abdomen revealed chronic atrophic calcific pancreatitis, with dilated pancreatic duct (7 mm) and intraductal calculi
Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) performed that confirmed the above findings
Hospitalized for pain control with narcotics
Admitted for management of pain secondary to chronic pancreatitis
CT scan performed in the emergency department revealed fatty atrophy of both the pancreatic head and uncinate process, calcifications in the pancreatic duct, and ductal dilatation of 1.5 cm
Transferred to the medical intensive care unit

Ultrasound of the abdomen showing a markedly dilated pancreatic duct (1.9 cm) with an 8.7 × 7.6 mm calculus, a diffusely echogenic pancreas, and a prominent common bile duct with no obvious obstructing calculi
Frank pus expelling spontaneously through the major papilla
No evidence of papillitis, tumor or previous sphincterotomy
Evacuation of more than 20 ml of pus from the pancreatic duct
Cholangiogram revealing a normal biliary tree without stones
Pancreatogram showed marked dilatation of the main pancreatic duct with a single distal calculus
A 5-cm-long 5 F stent was placed in the pancreatic duct and then removed
Acute suppuration of the pancreatic duct (ASPD) diagnosis

Re-evaluation with a contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen showing inflammatory changes in the fat around the body and tail of the pancreas, diminished ductal dilatation, distal migration of the calculus, and bilateral moderate pleural effusions, with no necrosis or fluid collection
Blood cultures grew Klebsiella ornithinolytica sensitive to the antibiotics given
Follow-up examination at 1 month after discharge revealed no further complications
Follow-up examination at 3 months after discharge revealed no further complications
